content.problemStatement:
There are **N** cards placed in a row, where every card has two numbers written on it, one on the top and 
one on the bottom. The numbers are between **1** and **N** (both inclusive). Every number is written on the top of exactly one card, and on the bottom of exactly one card as well.


Chef wants to rearrange the cards, such that the length of the longest common contiguous subsequence between the sequence formed by number written on top of the cards, and that formed by those written on the bottom is maximum. He can't modify the numbers written on any card and can't flip the cards, that is, for any card the number written on top remains at the top and the number written on bottom stays at the bottom. Find out the maximum possible length of the common contiguous subsequence.
constraints: All elements in both arrays A and B are distinct
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the number of cards. The next two lines contain N space-separated integers each, representing the numbers on the top and bottom of the cards.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the maximum length of the longest common contiguous subsequence.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, one possible arrangement is [1, 2, 3] and [2, 3, 1]. The longest common contiguous subsequence is [2, 3] with length 2. For the second test case, one possible arrangement is [7, 3, 2, 8, 6, 5, 4, 1] and [1, 5, 3, 2, 8, 6, 4, 7]. The longest common contiguous subsequence is [3, 2, 8, 6] with length 4.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use dynamic programming to find the longest common contiguous subsequence between the two sequences. We will create a 2D array dp where dp[i][j] represents the length of the longest common contiguous subsequence ending at index i in the first sequence and index j in the second sequence. We will then iterate over the two sequences and update the dp array accordingly. Finally, we will find the maximum value in the dp array, which will represent the maximum length of the longest common contiguous subsequence.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N^2) — where T is the number of test cases and N is the number of cards. The reason is that for each test case, we are iterating over the two sequences of length N, and for each pair of elements, we are updating the dp array.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N^2) — where N is the number of cards. The reason is that we are using a 2D array of size N x N to store the dp values.
